blocks.statement: I can apply V = IR to compute any one of V, I, or R given the other two; explain RESISTANCE as 'how hard charge has to push through' a component; and use the triangle to read off the formula.

blocks.whatWeKnow: From Day 4: voltage. From Day 5: current. These two are linked by RESISTANCE.
blocks.connection: Ohm's law is the master equation of every circuit — and the equation that lets us PREDICT the Car motor's current on Day 17 (given V and R).

blocks.markdown:
## The master equation

Voltage pushes; resistance pushes back; current is what results:

**V = I · R**

Resistance R measures how hard charge has to push through a component, in ohms (Ω). 1 Ω = 1 V/A. Crank up V and current rises; crank up R and current falls.

The three equivalent forms — pick the one that solves for your unknown:

| Want | Use |
|------|-----|
| Voltage | **V = I·R** |
| Current | **I = V/R** |
| Resistance | **R = V/I** |

The TRIANGLE trick: V on top, I and R on the bottom. Cover the unknown and read off the formula.

Feel for the numbers: copper wire ≈ 0.01 Ω (almost free) · a motor ≈ 3–5 Ω · a toaster element ≈ 15 Ω · an incandescent bulb ≈ 100 Ω. An open switch is R = infinity (no current); a short circuit is R ≈ 0 (huge current — dangerous).

terms.definition: How much a component opposes the flow of charge. R = V / I. Units: ohms (Ω). 1 Ω = 1 V/A. A copper wire: R ≈ 0.01 Ω (very low). A motor: R ≈ 3–5 Ω. An incandescent lightbulb: R ≈ 100 Ω. A toaster element: R ≈ 15 Ω (carries a lot of current).
terms.cognate: Sp. resistencia · Pt. resistência · HC rezistans

terms.definition: V = I · R. Equivalent forms: I = V/R, R = V/I. The TRIANGLE: cover the unknown, read the formula. 9V across a 3Ω motor → I = V/R = 9/3 = 3 A. Works for OHMIC materials — metals at constant temperature; bulbs and motors are not strictly ohmic (R changes with temperature), but we use it as an approximation.
terms.cognate: Sp. ley de Ohm · Pt. lei de Ohm · HC lwa Ohm

terms.definition: SI unit of resistance. 1 Ω = 1 V/A. Named for Georg Ohm. Common resistor values: 100 Ω, 1 kΩ, 10 kΩ, 1 MΩ — sized for the current and voltage of your circuit. (Capital omega Ω is 'ohm'; lowercase ω is angular frequency — different concept.)
terms.cognate: Sp. ohmio · Pt. ohm · HC ohm

blocks.title: High R means LITTLE current — and R ≈ 0 means danger
blocks.markdown: High R = LITTLE current at a given V (and vice versa). An open switch is R = infinity (no current at all). A short circuit is R ≈ 0 — Ohm's law then demands a HUGE current, which overheats wires. That's why shorts are dangerous.

blocks.title: The simplest circuit Ohm's law describes
blocks.caption: One loop: battery (the V), switch, resistor (the R). Close the switch and one current I = V/R flows around the whole loop. This is also EXACTLY the Car Project circuit — swap the resistor for a motor.

blocks.instruction: Draw the Ohm's triangle. Cover V → read V = IR. Cover I → read I = V/R. Cover R → read R = V/I. Practice each.
prompts: Triangle: V on top, I and R on the bottom.
Cover each letter in turn and write the formula you read.
Three formulas total — check them against the table.

blocks.prompt: CURRENT THROUGH A RESISTOR — A 6V battery is connected across a 12Ω resistor. What's the current?
RESISTANCE FROM V AND I — A circuit measures V = 9V across a component and I = 3 A through it. What's the resistance?
YOUR TURN — VOLTAGE ACROSS A COMPONENT. A current of 2.5 A flows through a 4 Ω resistor. What's the voltage across it?
blocks.equationHint: Cover the V on the triangle: V = I·R.

blocks.prompt: YOUR TURN — CAR MOTOR CURRENT PREVIEW. Estimate: the Car Project motor has R ≈ 4 Ω (estimated). Connected to a 9V battery, what current will it draw?
blocks.equationHint: Cover the I on the triangle: I = V/R. Expect a bit over 2 A.

blocks.title: Your first Car Project prediction
blocks.markdown: 9V / 4Ω = 2.25 A. That's the Car motor current PREDICTED from Ohm's law. Day 17 you'll do this prediction in full detail. Day 18 transfer task will ask you to predict again. Day 1 of Unit 8: you'll MEASURE.

blocks.prompt: A 12V battery drives 4 A through a wire-wound resistor. What's the resistance? Show one line of work. Then complete the frame.
blocks.frame: Ohm's law says V = ___. To find current, use I = ___. To find resistance, use R = ___. A 'short circuit' has R close to ___, leading to a ___ current.

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **34.5–34.10** (Ohm's law, electric shock, DC vs. AC, converting AC to DC, and how electrons actually move in a circuit). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.
